student_response: Settler territorial push influenced North America a good amount from 1754 to 1800, although empire wars maybe mattered just as much. After the French and Indian War Britain took land and the Proclamation tried to keep people east of the mountains but settlers still went. Later the new nation kept expanding and Native groups lost ground after fights like Fallen Timbers. Still Pinckney's deal with Spain opened the Mississippi so diplomacy shaped the map too. Overall settlers mattered alot but not alone.

Diffrent reform movements in the U.S reacted to industrilization from 1820 to 1900 in kinda opposite ways, some tried to fix morals and some tried to fight the factories directly. During this time alot of people left farms for cities looking for work and the living was crowded and dirty. Temperence groups said drinking made factory life worse and they pushed for local bans on liquor which shows how religous reform answered urban growth. Women at Seneca Falls also tied there push for rights to the chaos of market life and they wrote a declaration about equality even if factories were not the only reason. Abolitionists like Garrison and Douglass attacked slavery while cotton and mills were booming so that reform was reacting to the same commercial system that fed industry. Later labor unions like the Knights of Labor and strikes at Homestead and Haymarket tried to force better wages and shorter hours but bosses and courts often crushed them. Progressives and some congressmen also talked about child labor and filthy apartments near the mills. Carnegie preached giving money away while workers still got hurt which made reformers angry. So reform movements responded by moral campaigns, antislavery work, womens rights, and labor protest depending on who they thought industrilization was hurting most. In conclusino they didnt all agree but they all reacted to the new industrial order.

Land hunger ranked as the main cause of conflict between Europeans and Native Americans from 1500 to 1763, then religous conversion pressure, then fights over trade. Europeans claimed farms and mines while Native nations resisted shrinking hunting grounds. Missionaries tried to remake belifs which sparked anger. Competition over furs made violence worse, shown when tribes chose sides in the Seven Years War. Territory mattered most because other quarrels sat on who controlled the ground.

British settlers from 1607 to 1754 adapted to there enviornments in alot of ways. They grew crops. They traded with Native Americans. They built towns near rivers. Continuity and change both happened but its hard to seperate them cleanly. Jamestown started and people starved at first then tobacco worked in Virginia and that crop became how they survived and made money. New England was colder so they did fishing and small farms and ship stuff instead of big plantations. Middle colonies grew wheat and grain. The south grew tobacco and rice and indigo. Cotton too in some places. They adapted by using enslaved labor more and more especially after indentured servants seemed less reliable. Bacon's Rebellion happened and then slave codes got harsher. That is another adaptation. Native relations started sometimes helpful then turned violent like King Philips War in New England which changed how settlers lived on the frontier. They also used headrights to get land. Mercantilism and Navigation Acts made them ship goods to England which shaped what they planted. Continuity was always needing food and land. Change was going from struggling settlements to bigger export farms. Religious groups like Puritans adapted by building tight towns while southern planters spread out. The Great Awakening later shook churches but people still farmed the same soils. Scotch-Irish moved inland and farmed hills. Germans in Pennsylvania farmed too. Settlers adapted with different crops in different climates. They adapted with slavery. They adapted with trade. They adapted by pushing Native peoples off land. Continuity is they always wanted profit and survival. Change is the scale got bigger by 1754 and more enslaved people worked the land. Also assemblies and local government helped them manage ports and taxes. So adaptation was crops, labor, trade, and fighting over space listed over and over because that is mostly what happened.

Atlantic commerce remade British North American colonial society a large extent by 1776. Triangular trade and mercantilist rules tied colonies to England and Africa. Navigation Acts forced shipping patterns. In Virginia tobacco exports built a planter elite that depended on enslaved labor after the Middle Passage expanded. New England did shipbuilding and fish for markets. Those are real examples. Social order still had churches and local assemblies though so trade didnt erase everything. Overall commerce changed who held wealth and power especially in the South.
